slides.title: Introduction to Overfitting
slides.content: Overfitting occurs when a machine learning model learns the training data too well, capturing noise and exceptions. This results in poor performance on new, unseen data as the model fails to generalize.

slides.title: Identifying Overfitting
slides.content: A clear sign of overfitting is when a model performs exceptionally well on training data but poorly on validation or test data. This indicates the model has memorized rather than learned.

slides.title: Introduction to Regularization
slides.content: Regularization is a technique used to prevent overfitting by adding a penalty to the model's complexity. This encourages simpler models that generalize better to new data.

slides.title: L2 Regularization
slides.content: Also known as Ridge Regression, L2 Regularization adds a penalty equal to the square of the magnitude of coefficients. This discourages overly complex models.

slides.title: L1 Regularization
slides.content: L1 Regularization, or Lasso Regression, adds a penalty equal to the absolute value of the coefficients. It can lead to sparse models with some coefficients exactly zero.

slides.title: Conclusion
slides.content: Understanding and addressing overfitting is crucial for developing robust machine learning models. Regularization techniques like L1 and L2 help balance model complexity and generalization.
